perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Participant shall hold in confidence the Confidential Information, not disclose the Confidential Information except to Receiving Participant ’s Representatives in acc ordance with this Agreement, and will exercise the same amount of diligence in preserving the secrecy of that information as the diligence used in preserving the secrecy of the Receiving Participant’s Confidential Information of like importance, but in no event less than reasonable diligence.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Participant shall hold in confidence the Confidential Information, not disclose the Confidential Information except to Receiving Participant’s Representatives in acc ordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and will exercise the same amount of diligence in preserving the secrecy of that information as the diligence used in preserving the secrecy of the Receiving Participant’s Confidential Information of like importance, but in no event less than reasonable diligence,' the contract no longer requires reasonable measures to protect confidential information. This creates a legal risk, as the lack of a reasonable diligence clause could be viewed as insufficient protection of trade secrets under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By removing the requirement for 'reasonable diligence' in preserving confidentiality, the contract weakens the protection afforded to confidential information and could be found to be in conflict with CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for the Receiving Participant to exercise diligence in preserving the secrecy of confidential information, specifically eliminating the clause 'and will exercise the same amount of diligence in preserving the secrecy of that information as the diligence used in preserving the secrecy of the Receiving Participant’s Confidential Information of like importance, but in no event less than reasonable diligence.' According to the law explanation, CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Snippet 1, from California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' which includes the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent. The absence of a reasonable diligence clause weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with CUTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, making the contract non-compliant and creating a legal loophole.

perturbation.original_text: EXCEPT THAT, TO THE FULL EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW, THE RECEIVING PARTICIPANT SHALL N OT BE LIABLE FOR, AND HEREBY DISCLAIMS ANY LIABILITY FOR ANY DIRECT, INDIRECT, PUNITIVE, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES (INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF BUSINESS, REVENUE, PROFITS, GOODWILL, USE, DATA, OR OTHER ECONOMIC ADV ANTAGE) ARISING OUT OF, OR IN CONNECTION WITH INADVERTENT OR SUCH UNAUTHORIZED DISCLOSURE IF IT HAS EXERCISED THE SAME AMOUNT OF DILIGENCE IN PRESERVING THE SECRECY OF THAT INFORMATION AS THE DILIGENCE USED IN PRESERVING THE SECRECY OF THE RECEIVING PARTIC IPANT’S OWN CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION OF LIKE IMPORTANCE, BUT IN NO EVENT LESS THAN REASONABLE DILIGENCE, AND, UPON BECOMING AWARE OF SUCH DISCLOSURE, NOTIFIED THE DISCLOSING PARTICIPANT THEREOF AND TAKEN REASONABLE MEASURES TO MITIGATE THE EFFECTS OF SUCH DISCLOSURE AND TO PREVENT ANY FURTHER DISCLOSURE.
perturbation.changed_text: EXCEPT THAT, TO THE FULL EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW, THE RECEIVING PARTICIPANT SHALL N OT BE LIABLE FOR, AND HEREBY DISCLAIMS ANY LIABILITY FOR ANY DIRECT, INDIRECT, PUNITIVE, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES (INCLUDING, WITHOUT LIMITATION, LOSS OF BUSINESS, REVENUE, PROFITS, GOODWILL, USE, DATA, OR OTHER ECONOMIC ADV ANTAGE) ARISING OUT OF, OR IN CONNECTION WITH INADVERTENT OR SUCH UNAUTHORIZED DISCLOSURE.
perturbation.explanation: This omission removes the condition under which the Receiving Participant is NOT liable for damages related to unauthorized disclosure, specifically the requirement that they exercised reasonable diligence and took mitigating actions upon discovery of a breach. Without this condition, the disclaimer of liability becomes much broader, potentially conflicting with California's allowance of injunctive relief and actual damages for trade secret misappropriation, even in cases of inadvertent disclosure where reasonable protective measures weren't taken.
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2 allows for the recovery of damages for misappropriation of trade secrets.  By removing the requirement for the Receiving Participant to exercise reasonable diligence to avoid liability for inadvertent disclosure, the contract weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets and may violate California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the requirement for the Receiving Participant to exercise reasonable diligence and take mitigating actions upon discovery of unauthorized disclosure. This omission weakens the protection afforded to trade secrets, contradicting Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.2, which allows for injunctive relief and potentially damages for misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text provided a conditional limitation of liability contingent on the exercise of diligence, whereas the changed text removes this condition, creating a situation where the Receiving Participant could be shielded from liability even if they failed to take reasonable protective measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code Section 3426.2(a), explicitly allows for enjoining actual or threatened misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Participant shall have no obligation with respect to informat ion which: (i) was lawfully in the possession of or known to the Receiving Participant without any obligation of confidentiality prior to receiving it from the Disclosing Participant; TestFest NDA 2017 Confidential Page 6 of 8 (ii) is, or subsequently becomes, legally and publicly available oth er than by the Receiving Participant’s breach of this Agreement; (iii) becomes publicly available by action of the Disclosing Participant; (iv) is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Participant from a source other than the Disclosing Participant not un der any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Participant; (v) is developed by or for the Receiving Participant without use of the Confidential Information, provided such independent development can be shown by documentary evidence; or (vi) is disclosed by the Receiving Participant pursuant to a valid order issued by a court or government agency, provided that the Receiving Participant provides to the Disclosing Participant (a) prior written notice of such obligation, and (b) reasonable coope ration with Disclosing Participant’s efforts to limit or oppose such disclosure or obtain a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Participant shall have no obligation with respect to informat ion which: (i) was lawfully in the possession of or known to the Receiving Participant without any obligation of confidentiality prior to receiving it from the Disclosing Participant; TestFest NDA 2017 Confidential Page 6 of 8 (ii) is, or subsequently becomes, legally and publicly available oth er than by the Receiving Participant’s breach of this Agreement; (iii) becomes publicly available by action of the Disclosing Participant; (iv) is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Participant from a source other than the Disclosing Participant not un der any obligation of confidentiality to the Disclosing Participant; (v) is developed by or for the Receiving Participant without use of the Confidential Information, provided such independent development can be shown by documentary evidence; or (vi) is disclosed by the Receiving Participant pursuant to a valid order issued by a court or government agency.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'provided that the Receiving Participant provides to the Disclosing Participant (a) prior written notice of such obligation, and (b) reasonable coope ration with Disclosing Participant’s efforts to limit or oppose such disclosure or obtain a protective order' means that the receiving participant no longer has to provide prior notice or cooperate with the disclosing participant in limiting a disclosure mandated by a court order. This conflicts with the general principles of trade secret law, specifically the duty to protect trade secrets even when facing legal compulsion, potentially violating California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the Receiving Participant's obligation to provide prior notice and cooperate in limiting disclosure pursuant to a court order, the contract allows for potential trade secret misappropriation without reasonable attempts to mitigate the harm, potentially violating CUTSA's requirement of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Receiving Participant's obligation to provide prior notice and cooperate with the Disclosing Participant when disclosing confidential information pursuant to a court order. The Law Explanation states that this removal allows for potential trade secret misappropriation without reasonable attempts to mitigate harm, potentially violating CUTSA's requirement of reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' in CIV 3426.1 as the disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that their knowledge of the trade secret was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By removing the notification and cooperation requirements, the modified contract weakens the Disclosing Participant’s ability to protect their trade secrets when faced with a court order. This represents a direct and impactful contradiction, as it undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, making the contract potentially non-compliant with trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be govern ed by the laws of England without reference to conflict of laws principles and the courts of England shall have non -exclusive jurisdiction for all matters arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be govern ed by the laws of England without reference to conflict of laws principles and the courts of England shall have exclusive jurisdiction for all matters arising under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'non-exclusive jurisdiction' to 'exclusive jurisdiction' means that parties can *only* bring legal action in the courts of England. While not explicitly illegal in all situations, this can severely limit a California-based company's ability to pursue legal remedies under California law, particularly concerning trade secrets. It is possible to contract around CUTSA with a clear and unmistakable waiver of rights, but it must not violate public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: While a choice of law provision is generally enforceable, California courts will not enforce a provision that violates California public policy. The California Supreme Court has held that the protection of trade secrets is a strong public policy. A provision forcing a California company to litigate in England, where it may be difficult or impossible to enforce its CUTSA rights, could be deemed against public policy and unenforceable. This jurisdiction clause severely restricts the remedies available under CUTSA, potentially eliminating injunctive relief and other protections afforded by California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification changes 'non-exclusive jurisdiction' to 'exclusive jurisdiction,' which could force a California company to litigate trade secret disputes in England, potentially limiting its ability to enforce rights under CUTSA. The Law Explanation argues that California courts may deem such a provision against public policy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) as Civil Code sections 3426, indicating the existence of trade secret protections under California law. By mandating exclusive jurisdiction in England, the modified text could hinder or eliminate the California company's access to remedies available under CUTSA, creating a legal contradiction.
